request.question: What is the stage name of the singer who qualified to perform in the final round of Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015, along with  Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila?

title: Vaidas Baumila
Serbia in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Mia (singer)
Poland in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Monika Linkytė
This Time (Monika Linkytė & Vaidas Baumila song)
Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Macedonia in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Junior Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Australia in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
sentences: Vaidas Jacob Baumila (born 28 March 1987 in Vilnius) is a Lithuanian singer and actor.
He represented Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 along with Monika Linkytė with the song "This Time".
Serbia participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "Beauty Never Lies", written by Vladimir Graić and Charlie Mason.
The song was performed by Bojana Stamenov.

Serbian broadcaster Radio Television of Serbia (RTS) selected Vladimir Graić, the composer of the Eurovision Song Contest 2007 winning song "Molitva", to compose three candidate songs as potential Serbian entries for the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria.

Graić was also tasked with selecting the performers of these three entries, opting to selected two established Serbian artists, Bojana Stamenov and Aleksa Jelić, and one undiscovered talent, Danica Krstić, to perform the three entries.

RTS held the national final "Odbrojavanje za Beč" where the song "Ceo svet je moj" performed by Bojana Stamenov emerged as the winner after topping both the votes of a jury panel and a public televote.
The entry was later translated to English as "Beauty Never Lies" with lyrics written by Charlie Mason—the lyricist of Austria's Eurovision Song Contest 2014 winning song "Rise Like a Phoenix".
Vilija Pilibaitytė (born 19 July 1983), better known by her stage name Mia, is a Lithuanian singer, songwriter, and television host.
Poland participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "In the Name of Love", written by Kuba Raczyński and Monika Kuszyńska.
The song was performed by Monika Kuszyńska, who was selected by Polish broadcaster Telewizja Polska (TVP) to represent the nation at the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria.
The selection of Kuszyńska along with her song "In the Name of Love" were presented to the public in March 2015.
In the second of the Eurovision semi-finals "In the Name of Love" placed eighth out of the 17 participating countries, securing its place among the 27 other songs in the final.
In Poland's eighteenth Eurovision appearance on 23 May, "In the Name of Love" finished in twenty-third place, receiving 10 points.
Monika Linkytė (born 3 June 1992) is Lithuanian singer who represented Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 along with Vaidas Baumila with the song "This Time".
She also represented Lithuania in the "New Wave 2014".
Linkytė has previously attempted to represent Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest in 2010, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014 and in the Junior Eurovision Song Contest in 2007.
She was also a finalist on season two of "Lietuvos Balsas".
This Time (Monika Linkytė & Vaidas Baumila song)
Lithuania participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "This Time", written by Vytautas Bikus and Monika Liubinaitė.
The song was performed by Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila.

Lithuanian broadcaster Lithuanian National Radio and Television (LRT) organised the national final ""Eurovizijos" dainų konkurso nacionalinė atranka" in order to select the Lithuanian entry for the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria.
The national selection involved artists competing independently from their songs with separate selections running concurrently to choose the winning artist and song.
"This Time" was selected as the winning song from a field of twelve competing songs.
Three artists qualified to perform the winning song in the final round of the selection: Mia, Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila.

While Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila entered the competition performing as solo acts, they joined forces as a duet for the final artist selection and were subsequently selected as the performers for "This Time".
Macedonia participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "Autumn Leaves", written by Joacim Persson.
The song was performed by Daniel Kajmakoski.

In November 2014, Macedonian broadcaster Macedonian Radio Television (MRT) organised the "Skopje Festival 2014" in order to select the Macedonian entry for the 2015 Eurovision Song Contest in Vienna, Austria.
Daniel Kajmakoski and the song "Lisja esenski" emerged as the winner.
The song competed in Macedonia's national final in Macedonian with lyrics by Aleksandar Mitevski and Kajmakoski himself.
At the Eurovision Song Contest, however, Kajmakoski opted to perform the song in English under the title "Autumn Leaves".
In the first of the Eurovision semi-finals, Macedonia failed to qualify to the final, placing fifteenth out of the 16 participating countries with 28 points.
The Junior Eurovision Song Contest 2015 was the thirteenth annual Junior Eurovision Song Contest, and took place, for the first time, in Bulgaria.
The Bulgarian national broadcaster BNT was the host broadcaster for the event.
The final took place on 21 November 2015 and was held at the Arena Armeec in Sofia.
Poli Genova, a Bulgarian singer and former representative of Bulgaria in the Eurovision Song Contest 2011 and the Eurovision Song Contest 2016, hosted the show.
A total of seventeen countries participated, with Australia and Ireland making their debuts.
Albania and Macedonia returned after being absent since the 2012 and 2013 contests, respectively.
Croatia and Cyprus withdrew after returning in the 2014 edition , while Sweden withdrew for the first time since 2008.
Australia participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 in Vienna, Austria.
Owing to its cultural and political ties to European countries, the Eurovision Song Contest has been televised in Australia by the public broadcaster SBS for over thirty years.

In honour of the contest's 60th running and in recognition of the event's popularity in Australia, it was announced on 10 February 2015 that Australia was to be invited to compete in the 2015 contest as a special guest participant.
Australians have participated in and even won Eurovision as representatives of other countries.

In 2015 for example, the Australian coloratura soprano of Armenian descent Mary-Jean O'Doherty participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 contest as part of the group Genealogy representing Armenia.
But this marked the first time that the country has officially participated.

title: Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Mia (singer)

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Lithuania's national final for Eurovision Song Contest 2015 — finalists.

- Supported fact: Passage [1] lists "Three artists qualified ...: Mia, Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila." (quote: "Mia, Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila")
- Supported fact: Passage [1] states the selection was the national final ""Eurovizijos' dainų konkurso nacionalinė atranka"" (short quoted name).
- Ambiguity / missing fact: The passages do not give any additional identifying details (real name, biography) for "Mia" — label "Mia" as the bridge entity whose full identity is missing.
- Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
  1. No additional retrieval needed to confirm the stage name appears in the supplied passages ("Mia" is listed); defer final answer to downstream step.
  2. (If verification) Fetch "Mia (Lithuanian singer)" or search "Mia Lithuanian singer Eurovision 2015" — inspect lede/infobox and "Career" for real name and stage-name confirmation.
  3. Fetch "Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015" — section on "Eurovizijos' dainų konkurso nacionalinė atranka" / "Final" to confirm context and list of finalists.
  4. If "Mia" remains ambiguous, fetch "Mia (disambiguation)" or search "'Mia' Eurovision Lithuania 2015 finalist" for disambiguation.

While Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila entered the competition performing as solo acts, they joined forces as a duet for the final artist selection and were subsequently selected as the performers for "This Time".

Love (Sasha Son song) | "Love", known by its Lithuanian name "Pasiklydęs žmogus" ("The Lost Man") is a song by Lithuanian singer Sasha Son, the Lithuanian entry to the Eurovision Song Contest 2009, where it was performed in English with the final verse in Russian.

Måns Zelmerlöw | Måns Petter Albert Sahlén Zelmerlöw (] ; born 13 June 1986) is a Swedish pop singer and television presenter, winner in 2015 of the Eurovision Song Contest.  He took part in "Idol 2005", eventually finishing fifth, won the first season of "Let's Dance," and scored a hit with his 2007 song "Cara Mia", which was his entry in that year's Melodifestivalen.  Zelmerlöw was the host of "Allsång på Skansen" from 2011 to 2013.  He participated in Melodifestivalen in 2007, 2009 and won in 2015.  Zelmerlöw represented Sweden in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "Heroes", winning the contest with 365 points.  Zelmerlöw and Petra Mede co-hosted the Eurovision Song Contest 2016 held in Stockholm.

La mia città | "La mia città" (English: My city ) is a song by Italian singer Emma Marrone.  It was chosen by Italy's public broadcaster RAI to represent the country at the Eurovision Song Contest 2014.

Vaidas Baumila | Vaidas Jacob Baumila (born 28 March 1987 in Vilnius) is a Lithuanian singer and actor.  He represented Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 along with Monika Linkytė with the song "This Time".

Monika Linkytė | Monika Linkytė (born 3 June 1992) is Lithuanian singer who represented Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 along with Vaidas Baumila with the song "This Time".  She also represented Lithuania in the "New Wave 2014".  Linkytė has previously attempted to represent Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest in 2010, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014 and in the Junior Eurovision Song Contest in 2007.  She was also a finalist on season two of "Lietuvos Balsas".
